coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial photodynamic activity of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots and polycaprolactone based nanocomposite processed via both electrospinning and solvent casting method
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosal, Kajal
Kováčová, Mária
Humpolíček, Petr
Vajďák, Jan
Bodík, Michal
Špitalský, Zdenko
coredata.dc:description: Inhabitation of various types of bacteria on different surfaces causes vital health problems worldwide. In this work, a wound dressing defeating bacterial infection had been fabricated. The antibacterial effect of polycaprolactone and hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) based nanocomposite has been presented. The nanocomposite was fabricated both via solvent casting and electrospinning method. Nanocomposites with and without hCQDs had been investigated. A detailed study on their morphology and surface properties were performed by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy and Raman spectroscopy. Prepared nanocomposites had been evaluated by the contact angle, UV-Vis spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, and antibacterial activity. It was found that nanocomposites were able to produce singlet oxygen upon blue light irradiation at 470 nm, and they were effective in the eradication of Gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes) and Gram negative (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae) bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polycaprolactone
Carbon quantum dots
Electrospinning
Film casting
Antibacterial activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support of Ministry of Education of the Slovak Republic and Slovak Academy of Sciences , Grant VEGA 2/0051/20 . Authors Petr Humpolíček and Jan Vajďák acknowledge the project funded by the Czech Science Foundation ( 20-28732S ). The author Kajal Ghosal thanks SAIA for support of her visit at PISAS. The author also thank SERB-DST ( TAR/2020/000088 ) for supporting her research.

The authors are grateful for the financial support of Ministry of Education of the Slovak Republic and Slovak Academy of Sciences, Grant VEGA 2/0051/20. Authors Petr Humpolíček and Jan Vajďák acknowledge the project funded by the Czech Science Foundation (20-28732S). The author Kajal Ghosal thanks SAIA for support of her visit at PISAS. The author also thank SERB-DST (TAR/2020/000088) for supporting her research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs of (a–c) electrospun samples of F1, F2 and F3 respectively (high magnification).
SEM micrographs of (d–f) solvent casted films of F1, F2 and F3 respectively.
(a) The AFM scan of hCQDs spin-coated onto the pure mica substrate, (b) the distribution of the diameter of hCQDs, (c) the height distribution of hCQDs.

UV-VIS absorption spectra for solvent-casted films (black line corresponds to the film F1, red line to the film F2 and blue line to the film F3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) .
Raman maps of F1 film (a), F2 film (b), F3 film (c) and Raman spectrum (d) for solvent-casted film.
Mechanical strength of electrospun fibers (a) and solvent-casted films (b).
EPR spectra of films and fibers.

row.entry: Fibers with no beads and more homogenous

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sample both for electrospinning and solvent casting.
Antibacterial test for PCL films with hCQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: N means the number of viable bacteria recovered per cm2 per test specimen; R = the antibacterial activity where Ut is the average of the common logarithm of the number of viable bacteria (cells/cm2) recovered from the untreated test specimens, and At means the average of the common logarithm of the number of viable bacteria (cells/cm2) recovered from the treated test specimens.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial photodynamic activity of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots and polycaprolactone based nanocomposite processed via both electrospinning and solvent casting method

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inhabitation of various types of bacteria on different surfaces causes vital health problems worldwide. In this work, a wound dressing defeating bacterial infection had been fabricated. The antibacterial effect of polycaprolactone and hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) based nanocomposite has been presented. The nanocomposite was fabricated both via solvent casting and electrospinning method. Nanocomposites with and without hCQDs had been investigated. A detailed study on their morphology and surface properties were performed by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy and Raman spectroscopy. Prepared nanocomposites had been evaluated by the contact angle, UV-Vis spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, and antibacterial activity. It was found that nanocomposites were able to produce singlet oxygen upon blue light irradiation at 470 nm, and they were effective in the eradication of Gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes) and Gram negative (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae) bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: In 2014, Lord Jim O'Neill et al. published an estimation in a review that antimicrobial resistance could cause 10 million deaths a year by 2050 along with the economic risk of 100 trillion USD [1]. The burn is one of the serious causes causing infection. An infection that is due to burning leads to morbidity and mortality. Annual estimation is that there are annually 265 000 deaths due to burn infection worldwide, mostly in middle and low-income countries, whereas approximately 500 000 persons need treatment for burns in the United States, and in Europe, it is reported that there is the death of 25 000 persons due to fire. Skin, the largest organ of the human body, which prevents the entry of bacteria, viruses, maintains fluid equilibrium, body temperature and fluid equilibrium, along with the protection of internal tissues [2–4], get severely damaged after-burn. The severity of the damage depends on the degree of burn. The healing process of the skin is not an easy process rather consists of a complex consequence process. It is a sequence of hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation and remodeling. The first process takes place immediately whereas remodeling of the skin may need even a year [5–7]. The healing of the skin and prompt eradication of infection from the skin, is one of the frontline topics for investigation nowadays.

The ability of skin healing and infection treatment does not depend only on all endogenous healing process but also depend on wound dressing that may eradicate infection [8,9]. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop a novel wound dressing active against multidrug-resistant bacteria to heal burn-wound from within. From the ancient time, a myriad of dressings has been applied to cover up the burn infection. To date, there is a continuous effort to develop an ideal burn dressing material. They should preserve the moist environment at the burn site preventing microorganism entry and allowing gaseous exchange at the same time. They should help to get rid of excess exudates, be strong yet flexible, should attain similar biomimetic properties of extracellular matrix (ECM) and withstand in dynamic mechanical environments. They should be easily detached from the application site, be non-toxic and non-allergenic, must be anti-infective, cheap and suitable for skin tissue engineering.

3D scaffold obtained via electrospinning has drawn the attention of researchers for application in numerous fields such as in a biomedical field, drug delivery, energy, electrochemical sensing and environmental issues etc [10–12]. Fiber diameter from nanometer to submicron range, large surface-area-to-volume ratio, porosity, nature of material etc. of 3D electrospun scaffold make it suitable to mimic as an ECM which are essential features of a wound dressing scaffold [13]. As an antimicrobial agent, different types of antibacterial compounds are incorporated inside the electrospun fibers. Antibiotics are preferred compound but massive uses of them have developed strong resistance of microorganisms against them [14,15]. Nowadays also nanoparticles such as zinc oxide, titanium dioxide, silver, and gold analogs, have shown their potentials as antimicrobial agents. Along with the antibacterial properties, they can also modify chemical, thermal and mechanical properties of the electrospun scaffold. There are attempts to generate smart biomedical devices combatting pathogens causing infection to the burn wound along with tissue generation ability [16–20]. Researchers are also working on redox-active material that effectively kills deadliest pathogens like vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus etc.. Redox chemistry is ubiquitous, and one of nature's most effective strategies to combat pathogens [21,22]. One of the newest class of antibacterial materials of redox type is carbon quantum dots [23–25], and their polymer composites based on silicone [26] or polyurethanes [27,28] working after light activation.

In this paper, we have presented structural, mechanical and antibacterial properties of the electrospun wound dressing nanocomposite which was impregnated with antibacterial hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs). These kinds of wound dressing were also compared with hCQDs impregnated solvent casted polymer nanocomposite film. Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) are zero-dimensional redox-active materials with high chemical stability and low cost [29]. Polyesters which have wide applicability in the biomedical field are part of the main family of synthetic biodegradable polymers. Polycaprolactone (PCL) is one of them [30], used here for fabrication of nanocomposite film.

We have studied hCQDs/PCL nanocomposite for their antibacterial effect. A detailed study on their morphology and surface properties has been done by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Raman spectroscopy. The prepared nanocomposites have been also investigated for contact angle, UV-Visible spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR), and antibacterial efficiency. Significance of our work are mainly of threefold such as fabrication of a successful wound dressing scaffold, investigation of antibacterial activity of quantum DOTs and eradication of Gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes) and Gram negative (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae) bacteria.

The hCQDs were prepared according to the procedure, which was previously reported by Stanković et al. [25]. The hCQDs concentration in chloroform (purchased from Lach:ner s.r.o., Slovakia) was approximately 0.4 mg/mL. The polycaprolactone (PCL) (CAPA 6800, PCL, Mw= 80,000 g mol−1, melting point = 60°C, melt flow index (MFI) = 2.4 g/10 min (2.16 kg, 160°C), was obtained from Perstorp (Sweden). Methanol and ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich in p.a. purity.

Solutions of PCL for electrospinning were prepared in a mixture of chloroform and methanol in a ratio of 3:1. Three different concentrations of PCL (10% w/v, 12% w/v and 15% w/v, resp.) were prepared. At first, PCL of the weighted amount was added in the required solvent and continuously stirred for 2 h. Then the mixture was kept unstirred for another 15 min to remove the bubbles from the solution.

The selected solutions were electrospun from a 10 mL syringe with a metallic needle diameter of 0.41 mm at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/h. A high voltage of 9 – 25 kV was applied and the electrospun fibers were collected on aluminum foil. The counter electrode was placed 13 cm away from the needle tip. The process was carried out at ambient conditions.
For further optimization of the electrospinning process, the solvent ratio of chloroform and methanol was changed from 3:1 to 4:1. All the details have been provided in Table 1 .

PCL at 15% w/v concentration was prepared in the mixture of chloroform and methanol (4:1). Hydrophobic carbon quantum DOTs (hCQDs) at two different concentrations were added to PCL solution and stirred for 2 hrs for homogenous dispersion. Electrospinning process was performed to get a nanofibrous matrix.

Samples with the same concentration of hCQDs were prepared for the solvent casting method. The polymer solutions (without and with hCQDs) were placed into Petri dishes. All the Petri dishes were covered with an inverted funnel. The system was left overnight for air drying. After drying both electrospun and solvent casted nanocomposites were collected and stored in a desiccator until further use. Electrospun and solvent casted PCL films were also prepared without hCQDs. The formulation detail is given in Table 2 .

The surface morphologies of both solvent casted films and electrospun fibers were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on FIB Microscope Quanta 3D 200i in a secondary electron mode. Electrospun sample and the solvent-casted film were sectioned into specific length and width and then these sections were gold-coated using a Sample Preparation System Quorum Technologies Q150R S/E/ES.

Surface topography of hCQDs, electrospun samples, and solvent casted films were recorded by atomic force microscopy (AFM) in PeakForce Tapping mode on Multimode 8 microscope (Bruker, Germany) at room temperature in air. Root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of all samples was determined by Gwyddion software [31].

Contact angle measurements for both solvent casted films and electrospun samples were conducted to check the hydrophilicity. It was performed using Surface Energy Evaluation System (SEE System; Advex Instruments, s.r.o., Czech Republic) and its software. Both processing effects and also effects of addition hCQDs on hydrophilicity were investigated. Deionized water (3 µl droplets) was used for the measurement.

The polymer nanocomposite films and fibers with and without hCQDs were analyzed via UV-visible absorption spectra (Shimadzu UV−vis−NIR SolidSpec-3700 spectrophotometer) to verify the presence of hCQDs. The scanning range was fixed in between 600 – 370 nm.

The antibacterial testing of film casted samples was carried out on various G+ and G- strains, concretely the S. aureus CCM 4516, E. coli CCM 4517, K. pneumoniae CCM 4415, L. monocytogenes CCM 4699. These bacterial strains were collected from CCM (Czech Collection of Microorganisms). All samples were sterilized by ethanol shortly before testing. Antibacterial activity was conducted in accordance with ISO 22196 standard. The sample of dimension 25 × 25 × 1 mm3 was used. The used inoculum of S. aureus was 5.5 × 106 cfu/mL, E. coli 4.1 × 105 cfu/ml, K. pneumoniae 6.2 × 107 cfu/ml, and L. monocytogenes 1.2 × 107 cfu/ml. To investigate the effect of production of ROS by hCQDs on viability of bacterial strains, the samples were irradiated at 470 nm (power of 50 W) continued for 1 hour. The distance between the lamp and sample surface was maintained 50 cm for homogeneous illumination of the samples. Equations used for determination of the number of viable bacteria per cm2 per test specimen and calculation were done accordingly. Any specimen was tested out in triplicates for any bacterial strains.

The homogeneity of the electrospun fibers and nanocomposites were verified via large-area PL mapping by confocal Raman microscope (Alpha 300 R+, WiTec, Germany). The used green laser has an excitation wavelength of 532 nm and power 350 µW focused on the sample by 50 × (NA 0.8) objective. The signal was coupled to a spectrometer using optical fiber with a diameter of 100 µm. The fitting of the Raman spectra and construction of the Raman maps was done using the software provided with the instrument.

Electrospun fibers and solvent casted samples were characterized for mechanical strength by universal testing machine (Anton Paar; TS600). As the solvent casted films was very tough, tensile testing was performed using 600 N load. Specimens for the solvent casted samples were prepared with approximately width of 10 - 11 mm, the thickness of 0.22 mm and length of approximately 35 - 38 mm.

For electrospun fibers, the tensile strength experiment was carried out at a much lower load at 5 N. Fibrous samples were prepared using length of 10 - 13 mm, width of 8 - 9 mm and thickness of 0.20 mm.

By EPR method, singlet oxygen generation of PCL films/fibers with or without hCQDs had been investigated. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) had been used as a characteristic spin trap to react with 1O2 quickly which formed stable, EPR active product, TEMP-1O2(TEMPO). All six samples (3 films and 3 fibers) were dipped in ethanol solutions of TEMP and the concentration of TEMP was kept 2 wt%. The EPR experiments were done at room temperature using a Varian E-line spectrometer (UK). The spectrometer was operated at a frequency of 9.5 GHz, central field 3245 Gauss, gain 500, and power 1.00 mW. All samples were exposed to the blue light (BL) and irradiated during night respectively. The wavelength of BL was 470 nm.

Electrospinning is the very easiest way to get fibrous 3-dimensional matrix by using different synthetic and natural polymers. The concentration of the polymer should result in a viscous solution which can be stretched as fibers [32,33]. It was observed that 10% w/v concentration of our PCL solution at 9 kV voltage is not able to produce fibers, it only produced discrete like particles. Therefore, the concentration of polymer was increased from 10% to 12 % w/v and further to 15% w/v. By electrospinning, 12% w/v PCL solutions, PCL fibers were produced but fibers appeared with beads. The presence of beads depends on the voltage. Voltage is a very important processing parameter to be optimized to get fibers through the electrospinning process. Fibers will be only formed when threshold voltage will appear. This threshold voltage value generally is different for different polymer solution. At 12% w/v PCL concentration, different voltages were 9 kV, 12 kV, 15 kV, 20 kV and 25 kV. Here 25 kV voltage was forming fibers with very few numbers of beads but fibers were not homogenous. Same results were observed for 15% w/v PCL concentration at 9 kV, 12 kV, 15 kV, and 20 kV voltages. However, at a higher voltage (25 kV) and at a higher concentration of PCL (15% w/v), fibers appeared with almost no beads. Therefore, a slight change had been done for the solvent ratio. The amount of chloroform is slightly increased because the solubility of PCL fully depends on chloroform. It had been observed that with slight changes of the content of the solvent, homogenous fibers without any beads appeared (Table 1).
The voltage at 25 kV and 15% polymer concentration was chosen to be optimal for the electrospinning process with a solvent ratio of 4:1 (chloroform: methanol).

Fig. 1 (a–c) showed that fibers had a highly interconnected porous structure [34,35]. If electrospun fibers are aimed to be used as wound dressing or tissue scaffolds, it must mimic as ECM of living tissues by showing the network of interconnected pores [36]. Porosity is counted as one of the most important and crucial parameters for membranes to be used as skin tissue engineering scaffold. These three-dimensional electrospun meshes consisted of fibers with diameters varying from 20 nm to 3.5 μm with occasional extreme outliers having a diameter up to 3 - 4 μm were observed.

Pham et al. in 2006 had shown that the presence of both nanofibers and microfibers has a tremendous ability to work as a 3D tissue engineering scaffold [37]. Fibers without hCQDs had better regular structure than fibers with hCQDs. Fibers were formed from PCL with incorporated hCQDs but an insignificant amount of bead was seen.

SEM micrographs for solvent casted samples were also taken in order to know the effect of the process on the morphology of films. Fig. 2 (d–f) depicted SEM micrographs of the prepared PCL based solvent casted films. According to these images, it can be observed that keeping constant PCL concentration with increasing concentration of hCQDs (from 0, 0.228% w/w and 0.304% w/w) did not cause any changes in the morphology of the membranes. It may be due to that hCQDs were present in a minute amount. However, all solvent casted films were non-porous with the sudden appearance of big voids on the surface.

The electrospinning process offered sample with smooth morphology in contrast to that obtained from solvent-casting sample. Solvent casted nanocomposite films came up with an irregular surface and presence of crystals as slow evaporation of solvent occurred. The nodules in the solvent-casted samples are actually the crystallized domains.

The AFM was used to determine the average height and diameter of hCQDs. Fig. 3 a showed an AFM scan of hCQDs spin-coated onto a pure mica substrate. The average diameter (Fig. 3b) of hCQDs was 11.3±5.2 nm and the average height (Fig. 3c) of hCQDs is 1.1 nm.

In theory, both the composition and the geometrical structure of a surface control its wettability [38]. Hydrophobic groups are generally oriented at the air/solid interface whereas hydrophilic groups are present in the interior if the membrane has been fabricated by the electrospinning process [39]. The contact angle was found to be higher for electrospun fibers (Table 3 ). For solvent-casted films, the contact angle was found to be lower.

The geometrical structure of the solvent-casted surface might be influencing the wettability. Due to the slow evaporation rate of the solvent during the solvent-casting process, the films were smoother than the fibers. The surface roughness was probably the main cause of the difference in the water contact angle.

The encapsulation of the hCQDs into the solvent-casted films and electrospun fibers should change wettability than the films/fibers without hCQDs. As hCQDs are hydrophobic, their incorporation should increase contact angle, making the surface more hydrophobic. This was not the case for the solvent-casted films. Probably because the hCQDs were sufficiently well dispersed inside the polymer and their concentration at surface layer was very low. As a result, the contact value for the solvent-casted film was almost the same for the film with or without hCQDs. It varied from 85.2° to 89.6° whereas the values for fibers increased from 109.1⁰ to 119.6⁰, due to the smaller distance between the hydrophobic filler and the thin polymer fibers.

In the absorbance measurements (Fig. 4 ), there were no visible features on the spectra of pure or hCQDs-filled fibers. We can only observe a slight increase in the absorbance in the case of hCQDs-containing films.

The Raman map, in the case of F1 (Fig. 5 a), maps the intensity of the peak at 2900 rel. cm−1. We can see that the signal was homogeneous with random noise. For F2 and F3 (Fig, 5b and c) films the intensity of the fluorescence peak originating from hCQDs were mapped. The fluorescence was homogeneous within the resolution of our setup. In the case of F2 there was a small drop in the intensity in the center of the image. It was probably caused because of surface inhomogeneity.

From the spectrum of F1 (Fig. 5d, black) typical peaks of PCL polymer were seen. For F2 (Fig. 5d red) and F3 (Fig. 5d green) the fluorescence from hCQDs was detected along with the Raman signal. A strong and broad fluorescence peak and only weak Raman peak from the polymer at approx. 3500 rel. cm−1 was observed. The total fluorescence is stronger in the case of F3 (approx. 2 times stronger), because of the higher concentration of hCQDs.
The tensile stress vs. strain curves of different fibrous samples and solvent-casted films were plotted in Fig. 6 .

Investigations of mechanical properties reported that electrospun PCL membranes were softer with an excellent elongation rate [40]. The tensile strength for fibers varied in between 0.24 N/mm2 to 1.54 N/mm2 whereas for films, tensile strength varied from 9.55 N/mm2 to 11.87 N/mm2. If mechanical properties of solvent-casted films were compared with fibers, it can be seen that the critical elongation for fibers was almost 40 to 50%. In contrast, the critical elongation for films was only up to 5-10%. Tensile strength was higher for solvent-casted films than for fibers (Fig. 6). The incorporation of hCQDs in films or fibers increased the polymer's stiffness [41]. After the further increase of the hCQDs content, the mechanical strength decreased [42].

Pure PCL samples (F1 film/fiber) have not generated singlet oxygen. Fig. 7 shows EPR spectra of the following: TEMPO sample in BL for 24 h, F1, F2 and F3 (both films and fibers) in BL for 24 hr. As it was seen from the figure, that F2 and F3 samples generated singlet oxygen. F3 samples generated more singlet oxygen than the F2 sample.

On top of that, the amount of the generated singlet oxygen did not depend only on the amount of the hCQDs inside the polymer, but also on the preparation method. The solvent-casted films generated more singlet oxygen than the fibers (Fig. 7). It might be due to film casted samples consist of a higher amount of polymer composite than fiber sample in probed volume, due to the pore structure of fiber samples.

Because of the lower amount of singlet oxygen production for fiber samples, the solvent-casted films with the highest concentration of hCQDs were used to determine the antibacterial activity. To determine the application potential, the set of bacterial strains representing both the G+ and G-, were used. The results presented in Table 4 compare the amount of viable bacteria on the F3 with reference to F1. All specimens were irradiated by BL at 470 nm because the light with a wavelength longer than 405 nm cannot eradicate bacteria itself and therefore did not create a false positive result [43]. Incorporation of hCQDs into PCL films increased its antibacterial activity towards all used bacterial species. In the case of S. aureus, E. coli and L. monocytogenes the antibacterial activity was especially significant. In case of K. pneumoniae was the effect lower. It is important to notice, that observed antibacterial activity was observed even despite the fact, that specimen was irradiated only from upper side. In accordance to previously published results we can conclude, that bacterial death occurred due to the generation of reactive oxygen species from quantum DOTs, which damaged the bacterial cell wall [44,45]. The results clearly show that prepared wound dressing can be efficiently used for elimination of bacterial contamination.

This study reported comparison PCL nanocomposites filled with hydrophobic CQDs prepared by two different techniques – by electrospinning and by solvent casting method. The instrumental analysis confirmed the incorporation of hCQDs inside both samples. The solvent-casted films showed lower contact angles than the electrospinning membrane that was mainly due to a geometrical structure of the surface, however, solvent casted nanocomposites were tougher and produced more singlet oxygen than electrospun samples with the same concentration of filler. The antibacterial test confirmed the antibacterial effect against G+ (S. aureus and L. monocytogenes) and G- (E. coli and K. pneumoniae) bacteria.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of Ministry of Education of the Slovak Republic and Slovak Academy of Sciences, Grant VEGA 2/0051/20. Authors Petr Humpolíček and Jan Vajďák acknowledge the project funded by the Czech Science Foundation (20-28732S). The author Kajal Ghosal thanks SAIA for support of her visit at PISAS. The author also thank SERB-DST (TAR/2020/000088) for supporting her research.
